summary: Joel Spolsky argues that software schedules are essential but widely avoided because programmers find them painful and unreliable. He presents a 13-step method for creating accurate, low-overhead schedules using simple Excel spreadsheets. The method centers on breaking features into fine-grained tasks (2-16 hours each), having only the implementing programmer estimate their own work, and tracking original vs. current estimates to improve over time. He emphasizes that realistic scheduling forces teams to cut low-value features early, resulting in better products that ship on time. The post uses cautionary tales from Netscape, Lotus, and Amtrak to illustrate the catastrophic costs of not having or not following a schedule.
key_points: Breaking tasks into fine-grained items (2-16 hours) produces fundamentally different and more accurate estimates than rough chunky tasks, because it forces you to actually design the feature and think through implementation steps.
Only the programmer doing the work should estimate it — management-imposed estimates are fantasy, and pressuring programmers to shrink estimates backfires through burnout and doubled debugging time.
Schedules must include buffer, debugging (100-200% of coding time), integration, vacations, and holidays — these non-coding items often exceed the actual task estimates.
The real benefit of scheduling is that it forces you to cut features, which paradoxically produces better products by eliminating low-value work that would otherwise consume time.

Managers who try to motivate through unrealistically tight schedules are counterproductive — being behind schedule makes programmers feel doomed and unmotivated, while being ahead makes them productive.
money_quotes: When you think of writing code without thinking about all the steps you have to take, it always seems like it will take n time, when in reality it will probably take more like 3n time.
But you can never get 3n from n, ever, and if you think you can, please email me the stock ticker of your company so I can short it.
A schedule is like wood blocks. If you have a bunch of wood blocks, and you can't fit them into a box, you have two choices: get a bigger box, or remove some blocks.
When I'm behind schedule, I feel doomed and depressed and unmotivated. When I'm working ahead of schedule, I'm cheerful and productive. The schedule is not the place to play psychological games.
You might be able to get 10% more raw code out of people temporarily at the cost of having them burn out 100% in a year. Not a big gain, and it's a bit like eating your seed corn.
If you have a hand-wavy feature called 'Internet Integration' and you schedule 3 weeks for it, you are doomed, buddy.
Programmers are not interchangeable. It takes seven times longer for John to fix Rita's bug than for Rita to fix Rita's bug.
We were absolutely shocked to see that the list of cut features was the shoddiest list of features you could imagine. Not one of those features was worth doing.
key_insight: Fine-grained task estimation by the implementing programmer produces fundamentally more accurate schedules than rough estimates, and the discipline of scheduling forces teams to cut low-value features, resulting in better products.
